chunks.text: Trevor Darrell is a Professor in Residence in the CS Division at UC Berkeley. His group develops algorithms for visual recognition across platforms and applications. His interests include computer vision, machine learning, computer graphics, and perception-based human interfaces. He was on the MIT EECS faculty from 1999-2008, directing the Vision Interface Group. He worked at Interval Research Corporation from 1996-1999. He earned his S.M. and Ph.D. from MIT in 1992 and 1996, and his B.S.E. from the University of Pennsylvania in 1988, starting in computer vision as an undergraduate researcher in Ruzena Bajcsy's GRASP lab.

Trevor Darrell's education includes a PhD from MIT in 1996 and a BSE from the University of Pennsylvania in 1988. His research areas are Artificial Intelligence (AI), Control, Intelligent Systems, and Robotics (CIR), and Computer Vision. He is affiliated with several research centers: Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR), Berkeley Center for Responsible, Decentralized Intelligence (RDI), Berkeley Deep Drive (BDD), CITRIS People and Robots (CPAR), International Computer Science Institute (ICSI), and Partners for Advanced Transit and Highways (PATH).

Trevor Darrell teaches CS 294-194: Research to Startup on Thursdays from 17:00-18:29 in Soda 310. Selected publications include 'Dynamic Feature Selection for Classification on a Budget' (ICML-W 2013), 'Timely Object Recognition' (NIPS 2012), and 'Discovering Latent Domains for Multisource Domain Adaptation' (ECCV 2012). Selected honors and awards include the ICML Test of Time Award (2024), ACM SIGMM Test of Time Paper Award (2024), CS TCPAMI CVPR Longuet-Higgins Prize (2024), CS TCPAMI ICCV Helmholtz Prize (2017), CS PAMI Mark Everingham Prize (2017), and RAS ICRA Best Paper Award in Cognitive Robotics (2013).
